title: CIL Mechanical — Advanced Mock 11 (Conceptual)
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: As per Income Tax Department data (June 2026), India's net direct tax collections in early FY27 rose by about what percentage year-on-year?
questions.solution: Net direct tax collections rose ~14.6% YoY to about Rs 5.21 trillion.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, which country became the first Central Asian member of the New Development Bank (NDB)?
questions.solution: Uzbekistan became the first Central Asian nation to join the BRICS-backed NDB.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which India-born theoretical physicist won the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics?
questions.solution: Rajasthan-born physicist Jainendra K. Jain received the 2025 Wolf Prize in Physics.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which institution ranked first in the QS World University Rankings 2027 (released June 2026)?
questions.solution: Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) ranked first in QS World University Rankings 2027.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In June 2026, which Indian jurist was elected a Judge of the International Tribunal for the Law of the Sea (ITLOS)?
questions.solution: Indian jurist Bimal N. Patel was elected to ITLOS in June 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, PM Narendra Modi received the highest national award of which country?
questions.solution: PM Modi was conferred Slovakia's highest national award in 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026, in which an Indian Army contingent participated, was held in which country?
questions.solution: The multinational Exercise KHAAN QUEST 2026 was held in Mongolia.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who was named the new Chairman of GIC Re in June 2026?
questions.solution: The government named Hitesh Joshi as the new Chairman of GIC Re.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: In 2026, ONGC was assigned to develop a new strategic petroleum reserve (SPR) facility at which location?
questions.solution: ONGC was tasked with building a new underground crude oil SPR cavern at Mangaluru, Karnataka.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The India–UK CETA and Social Security Agreement were reported to take effect from which date?
questions.solution: Reports indicated the India–UK CETA would take effect from July 15, 2026.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the chemical symbol of Gold?
questions.solution: Gold's symbol is Au (from Latin 'aurum'); Ag is silver.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the chemical symbol of Sodium?
questions.solution: Sodium's symbol is Na (from Latin 'natrium'); Sn is tin.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The most abundant metal in the Earth's crust is:
questions.solution: Aluminium is the most abundant metal in the Earth's crust.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who chaired the Drafting Committee of the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution: Dr. B. R. Ambedkar chaired the Drafting Committee of the Constitution.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who was the first President of India?
questions.solution: Dr. Rajendra Prasad was the first President of India (1950–62).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: How many Fundamental Rights are currently guaranteed by the Indian Constitution?
questions.solution: There are six Fundamental Rights after the Right to Property was removed in 1978.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Directive Principles of State Policy are enshrined in which Part of the Constitution?
questions.solution: Directive Principles are contained in Part IV (Articles 36–51).

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Who is the constitutional head of the Indian Union?
questions.solution: The President of India is the constitutional head of the Union.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the national animal of India?
questions.solution: The Royal Bengal Tiger is the national animal of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the national bird of India?
questions.solution: The Indian Peafowl (peacock) is the national bird of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the national flower of India?
questions.solution: The lotus is the national flower of India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is the longest river in India?
questions.solution: The Ganga is the longest river flowing within India.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Which is the highest mountain peak entirely within India?
questions.solution: Kangchenjunga is the highest peak located within India's borders.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: The Tropic of Cancer passes through how many Indian states?
questions.solution: The Tropic of Cancer passes through eight Indian states.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What is the currency of Japan?
questions.solution: The Yen is the currency of Japan; Yuan is Chinese, Won is Korean.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A invests Rs 3000 for 12 months and B invests Rs 6000 for 6 months. Out of a total profit of Rs 4500, A's share is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Profit is split in the ratio of capital × time invested by each partner.
**Formula.** $\text{share}_A=P\cdot\tfrac{x\,m_A}{x\,m_A+y\,m_B}$
**Given.** A: Rs 3000×12 mo; B: Rs 6000×6 mo; profit Rs 4500.
**Working.** Ratio $=36000:36000$; share$_A=4500\times\tfrac{36000}{72000}$.
**Answer.** A's share $=$ Rs 2250.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A boat covers 140 km downstream in 10 h and the same 140 km upstream in 14 h. Its speed in still water is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Downstream speed adds the stream; upstream subtracts it. Still-water speed is the average of the two.
**Formula.** $u=\tfrac12\left[(b+s)+(b-s)\right]$
**Given.** Downstream 140 km in 10 h; upstream 140 km in 14 h.
**Working.** Downstream $=\tfrac{140}{10}=14$ km/h, upstream $=\tfrac{140}{14}=10$ km/h.
**Answer.** Still-water speed $u=\tfrac{14+10}{2}=12$ km/h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A pipe fills a tank in 8 h, but with a leak it takes 9 h. The leak alone can empty the full tank in:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The leak's rate is the fill-rate without the leak minus the slower fill-rate with the leak.
**Formula.** $T=\tfrac{ab}{b-a}$
**Given.** Fill without leak a = 8 h; with leak b = 9 h.
**Working.** Leak rate $=\tfrac1{8}-\tfrac1{9}$; emptying time $=\tfrac{8\times9}{9-8}$.
**Answer.** $T=72$ h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A trader marks goods 50% above cost, then gives a 10% discount. His net profit percentage is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Net selling factor is the markup factor times the discount factor; profit% is that factor reduced by 1.
**Formula.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{m}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{d}{100}\right)$
**Given.** Markup m = 50%, discount d = 10%.
**Working.** $f=\left(1+\tfrac{50}{100}\right)\left(1-\tfrac{10}{100}\right)=1.35$.
**Answer.** Profit $=(f-1)\times100=35\%$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Two trains 150 m and 100 m long move in opposite directions at 72 km/h and 36 km/h. The time to cross each other is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Opposite directions add speeds; convert km/h to m/s with the factor 5/18, then divide the combined length by the relative speed.
**Formula.** $t=\tfrac{L_1+L_2}{(s_1+s_2)\times\frac{5}{18}}$
**Given.** L₁ = 150 m, L₂ = 100 m, s₁ = 72 km/h, s₂ = 36 km/h.
**Working.** Relative $=(72+36)\times\tfrac{5}{18}=30$ m/s; $t=\tfrac{150+100}{30}$.
**Answer.** $t=8.33$ s.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The angle between the hour and minute hands of a clock at 2:20 is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** The hour hand sweeps 30° per hour and 0.5° per minute, the minute hand 6° per minute; take the smaller of the two angles.
**Formula.** $\theta=\left|30H-5.5M\right|$
**Given.** H = 2, M = 20.
**Working.** $=\left|30\times2-5.5\times20\right|=50^\circ$; smaller $=\min(50,360-50)$.
**Answer.** $\theta=50^\circ$.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A vessel holds 80 L of milk. 10 L is drawn out and replaced with water; this is repeated 2 times. The milk remaining is about:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Each draw-and-replace keeps the fraction $(1-x/V)$ of milk; after n repeats this fraction is raised to the n-th power.
**Formula.** $M=V\left(1-\tfrac{x}{V}\right)^{n}$
**Given.** V = 80 L, x = 10 L, n = 2.
**Working.** $=80\left(1-\tfrac{10}{80}\right)^{2}$.
**Answer.** $M\approx 61.25$ L.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The difference between compound and simple interest on Rs 5000 at 10% per annum for 2 years is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Over 2 years the gap between compound and simple interest is the first year's interest-on-interest.
**Formula.** $CI-SI=P\left(\tfrac{r}{100}\right)^2$
**Given.** P = Rs 5000, r = 10% p.a., t = 2 yr.
**Working.** $=5000\left(\tfrac{10}{100}\right)^2$.
**Answer.** $CI-SI=$ Rs 50.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A invests Rs 3000 for 6 months and B invests Rs 8000 for 4 months. Out of a total profit of Rs 4500, A's share is:
questions.solution:
**Concept.** Profit is split in the ratio of capital × time invested by each partner.
**Formula.** $\text{share}_A=P\cdot\tfrac{x\,m_A}{x\,m_A+y\,m_B}$
**Given.** A: Rs 3000×6 mo; B: Rs 8000×4 mo; profit Rs 4500.
**Working.** Ratio $=18000:32000$; share$_A=4500\times\tfrac{18000}{50000}$.
**Answer.** A's share $=$ Rs 1620.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A can do a work in 18 days and B in 30 days. Working together they finish it in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: What is the average of the first 12 natural numbers?
questions.solution: Average of first n naturals = (n+1)/2 = (12+1)/2 = 6.5.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The average age of 4 persons is 30 years. If a person aged 42 leaves, the average of the remaining 3 is:
questions.solution: Total = 120; remaining = 120 − 42 = 78; average = 26.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The LCM of 10 and 15 is:
questions.solution: The lowest common multiple of 10 and 15 is 30.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A man goes at 40 km/h and returns over the same route at 60 km/h. His average speed is:
questions.solution: Average speed = 2uv/(u+v) = 2×40×60/(40+60) = 48 km/h.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A car covers a distance at 50 km/h in 4 hours. The distance is:
questions.solution: Distance = speed × time = 50 × 4 = 200 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A number divided by 7 gives quotient 12 and remainder 3. The number is:
questions.solution: Number = divisor × quotient + remainder = 7×12+3 = 87.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A sum doubles itself in 8 years under simple interest. The annual rate is:
questions.solution: To double, SI = P, so rate = 100/T = 100/8 = 12.5%.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The HCF of 24 and 60 is:
questions.solution: The highest common factor of 24 and 60 is 12.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: What is 15% of 640?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: An article sold at Rs 352 incurs a 12% loss. Its cost price is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The square root of 5625 is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: An item bought for Rs 500 is sold for Rs 600. The profit percentage is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The percentage increase when a value rises from 80 to 95 is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: The compound ratio of 4 : 3 and 5 : 7 is:
questions.solution: Compound ratio = (4×5) : (3×7) = 20 : 21.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: 3 kg of rice at Rs 30/kg is mixed with 6 kg at Rs 50/kg. The average price per kg is:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A = 1, B = 2, … , Z = 26, what is the sum of the letter values of 'ACE'?
questions.solution: Add the positional values of A, C, E to get 9.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term in the series: 5, 11, 23, 47, 95, ?
questions.solution: Each term = previous × 2 + 1, so the next term = 95 × 2 + 1 = 191.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a certain code, each letter of a word is moved two steps forward in the English alphabet. The code for 'DOG' is:
questions.solution: Replace every letter by the one two places ahead: DOG → FQI.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Pointing to a man, a woman said, 'His mother is the only daughter of my mother.' How is the woman related to the man?
questions.solution: Tracing each relationship step by step gives: Mother.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some cats are dogs. All dogs are animals. Which conclusion necessarily follows?
questions.solution: Applying the rules of categorical syllogism, the valid conclusion is: Some cats are animals.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A man walks 9 km towards north, then turns east and walks 12 km. How far is he from the starting point?
questions.solution: Straight-line distance = √(9² + 12²) = √225 = 15 km.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a certain code, each letter of a word is moved two steps forward in the English alphabet. The code for 'TREE' is:
questions.solution: Replace every letter by the one two places ahead: TREE → VTGG.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is the brother of B. B is the brother of C. C is the father of D. How is A related to D?
questions.solution: Tracing each relationship step by step gives: Uncle.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If A=1, B=2, ... then the sum of the letters of BED is:
questions.solution: Add the alphabet positions of B, E, D to get 11.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: 8, 27, 50, 64?
questions.solution: The others are perfect cubes; 50 is not.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If each letter is replaced by the next letter, BIRD is coded as:
questions.solution: Shift each letter forward by one to get CJSE.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: The angle between the hands of a clock at 2:00 is:
questions.solution: Each hour = 30°; at 2:00 the hands are 2 × 30 = 60° apart.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A person facing north turns 90° to the right. Now they face:
questions.solution: Turning right from north leads to East.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: M, N, O, P, ?
questions.solution: Consecutive alphabet letters; after P comes Q.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: AB, DE, GH, ?
questions.solution: Each pair skips one letter; after GH comes JK.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: X, V, T, R, ?
questions.solution: Letters decrease by 2 each time, so the next letter is P.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which is the odd one out: Copper, Iron, Oxygen, Zinc?
questions.solution: Oxygen belongs to a different category from the rest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Hand : Glove :: Foot : ?
questions.solution: Just as Glove relates to Hand, Sock relates to Foot.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.solution: Each number is halved, so 24 → 12.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 4, 9, 16, 25, ?
questions.solution: These are perfect squares; next = 6² = 36.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the next term: 4, 7, 13, 22, ?
questions.solution: Differences grow by 3; adding the next difference gives 34.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: All pens are tools. All tools are useful. Which conclusion follows?
questions.solution: Both premises are universal affirmatives, so the chained conclusion 'All pens are useful' follows.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a row of 20 people, X is 8th from the left. X's position from the right is:
questions.solution: Position from right = 20 − 8 + 1 = 13.

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Which of these fractions is the largest?
questions.solution: Comparing decimal values, 4/5 is the largest.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'He is married ___ a doctor.'
questions.solution: The correct collocation is 'married to' someone.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'The book is ___ the table.'
questions.solution: 'On' indicates resting on a surface.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One word for 'a person who cannot read or write':
questions.solution: Such a person is 'illiterate'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The study of living organisms is called:
questions.solution: 'Biology' is the study of all living organisms.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One who studies stars and celestial bodies is an:
questions.solution: An 'astronomer' scientifically studies celestial bodies.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: One word for 'a speech made without preparation':
questions.solution: An unprepared speech is delivered 'extempore'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: A place where birds are kept is called:
questions.solution: An 'aviary' houses birds; an apiary keeps bees.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to break the ice' means:
questions.solution: 'Break the ice' means to initiate conversation in a tense or new setting.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'a piece of cake' means:
questions.solution: 'A piece of cake' means something very easy to do.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'once in a blue moon' means:
questions.solution: 'Once in a blue moon' means something happening very rarely.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to bury the hatchet' means:
questions.solution: 'Bury the hatchet' means to end a quarrel and make peace.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The idiom 'to let the cat out of the bag' means:
questions.solution: It means to accidentally reveal a secret.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Child' is:
questions.solution: 'Child' has the irregular plural 'children'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The plural of 'Mouse' is:
questions.solution: 'Mouse' has the irregular plural 'mice'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Neither he nor I ___ wrong.'
questions.solution: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the nearer subject 'I', so 'am'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'Each of the students ___ a book.'
questions.solution: 'Each' is singular, so it takes 'has'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'He is ___ honest man.'
questions.solution: 'Honest' starts with a vowel sound, so 'an' is used.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Fill in the blank: 'She plays ___ violin beautifully.'
questions.solution: Musical instruments take the definite article 'the'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'John wrote the letter.' is:
options: The letter was written by John
The letter is written by John
questions.solution: Past simple active becomes 'was written' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Passive voice of 'She is reading a book.' is:
options: A book was read by her
A book is read by her
A book is being read by her
A book has been read by her
questions.solution: Present continuous active becomes 'is being read' in the passive.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'He / don't / like / coffee.'
questions.solution: Third-person singular needs 'doesn't', not 'don't'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Spot the error: 'One of the boys / are / playing / outside.'
questions.solution: 'One of the boys' is singular, so it requires 'is'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'I ___ my homework already.'
questions.solution: 'Already' signals present perfect, so 'have done' is correct.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the correct form: 'By next year, she ___ here for a decade.'
questions.solution: A completed action by a future point uses the future perfect 'will have worked'.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Choose the word that means the opposite of 'Artificial'.
questions.solution: 'Artificial' is opposite to 'natural'.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A material that undergoes large plastic deformation before fracture is described as:
questions.solution: Ductile materials (e.g. mild steel) show significant elongation before failure.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Young's modulus is the ratio of:
questions.solution: E = σ/ε for axial loading within the elastic limit.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The gauge pressure at a depth of 5 m in water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The moment of inertia of a rectangular section (width b, depth d) about its centroidal horizontal axis is:
questions.solution: I = bd³/12 about the centroidal axis parallel to the width.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 2 kg body moves at 4 m/s. Its kinetic energy is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a constant-volume (isochoric) process the work done by a closed system is:
questions.solution: W = ∫p dV = 0 when volume is constant, so all heat changes internal energy.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A long, slender column under axial compression is most likely to fail by:
questions.solution: Slender columns fail by elastic buckling (Euler) well before the crushing stress is reached.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The gauge pressure at a depth of 10 m in water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Resilience of a material refers to its capacity to absorb energy:
questions.solution: Resilience = strain energy stored up to the elastic limit; toughness is up to fracture.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water issues from an orifice under a head of 20 m. The ideal jet velocity (Torricelli) is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Brayton cycle is the ideal cycle for a:
questions.solution: The Brayton (Joule) cycle models gas-turbine plants with constant-pressure heat addition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Clausius inequality ∮ dQ/T ≤ 0 is satisfied with the equality sign only for:
questions.solution: ∮dQ/T = 0 for reversible cycles and < 0 for irreversible ones.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An axial load of 10 kN acts on a bar of 100 mm² cross-section. The normal stress is:
questions.solution: σ = F/A = 10kN/100mm² = 100 MPa.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Comparing engines between identical temperature limits, which is true?
options: No real engine can exceed the Carnot efficiency
Carnot efficiency depends on the working fluid
A real engine can exceed Carnot if well designed
All engines have the same efficiency
questions.solution: Carnot efficiency is the upper bound and is independent of the working substance.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the cantilever shown (end load W = 5 kN, length L = 2 m), the maximum bending moment (at the fixed end) is:
questions.solution: For a cantilever with an end load, M_max = WL = 5×2 = 10 kN·m at the fixed end.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The gauge pressure at depth h in a liquid of density ρ is:
questions.solution: Hydrostatic pressure p = ρgh increases linearly with depth.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The zeroth law of thermodynamics defines the concept of:
questions.solution: If two bodies are each in thermal equilibrium with a third, they are with each other — the basis of temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A heat exchanger has end temperature differences of 60 K and 20 K. The LMTD is about:
questions.solution: LMTD = (ΔT₁ − ΔT₂)/ln(ΔT₁/ΔT₂) = (60−20)/ln(60/20) ≈ 36.4 K.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The main function of a flywheel is to:
options: Reduce fluctuation of speed over a cycle
Control mean speed for load change
questions.solution: A flywheel stores/returns energy to smooth speed fluctuations; a governor controls mean speed.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 500 mm bar elongates by 1 mm under load. Its strain (×10⁻³) is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ratio of specific heats γ for a gas equals:
questions.solution: γ = Cp/Cv; for air ≈ 1.4.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Brinell and Rockwell tests are used to measure a material's:
questions.solution: Both are indentation hardness tests.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A heat exchanger has end temperature differences of 100 K and 30 K. The LMTD is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A throttling (Joule–Thomson) process across a valve is characterised by constant:
questions.solution: Adiabatic throttling with negligible KE/PE keeps h₁ = h₂; temperature usually changes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the simply supported beam shown (central load W = 10 kN, span L = 4 m), the maximum bending moment is:
questions.solution: For a central point load, M_max = WL/4 = 10×4/4 = 10 kN·m.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><line x1="30" y1="60" x2="270" y2="60" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="3"/><polygon points="30,60 22,78 38,78" fill="none" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><polygon points="270,60 262,78 278,78" fill="none" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="150" y1="20" x2="150" y2="58" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="150,58 145,48 155,48" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="150" y="16" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#dc2626">W=10kN</text><text x="150" y="96" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">span L=4 m (load at mid-span)</text></svg>
figure.caption: Simply supported beam, central load

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The air-standard efficiency of the Otto cycle with compression ratio r is:
questions.solution: η_Otto = 1 − 1/r^(γ−1); efficiency rises with compression ratio.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an adiabatic process the quantity that is zero is:
questions.solution: Adiabatic ⇒ Q = 0; the gas may still do work and change temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A reversed Carnot refrigerator works between 300 K and 260 K. Its COP is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the same machine, the COP of a heat pump and that of a refrigerator are related by:
questions.solution: Heat rejected = heat absorbed + work, so COP_HP = COP_ref + 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A refrigerator extracts 300 W of heat using 100 W of work. Its COP is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Reynolds number represents the ratio of:
questions.solution: Re = ρVD/μ = inertia/viscous; it indicates laminar vs turbulent flow.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: If the depth of a rectangular beam is doubled (same width and load), its bending strength (resisting moment) increases by a factor of:
questions.solution: Section modulus Z = bd²/6 ∝ d², so the moment capacity rises 2² = 4×.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the p–V diagram shown, two expansions start from the same state (V₁ ≈ 2). The steeper curve (1) is the:
questions.solution: Adiabatic curves (pV^γ) are steeper than isothermal curves (pV) on a p–V diagram because γ > 1.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M50 30 C 90 70 140 100 220 112" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M50 45 C 95 85 150 108 220 118" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/><text x="150" y="60" font-size="9" fill="#2563eb">curve 1 (steeper)</text><text x="150" y="100" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">curve 2 (flatter)</text><text x="232" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#475569">V</text><text x="34" y="14" font-size="10" fill="#475569">p</text></svg>
figure.caption: p–V diagram: adiabatic vs isothermal

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For a circular shaft in torsion, the relation T/J = τ/r = Gθ/L is the:
questions.solution: The torsion equation links torque, polar moment J, shear stress and twist.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the tensile stress–strain curve shown for mild steel (yield ≈ 250 MPa), point A (end of the initial straight line) represents the:
questions.solution: Point A is the yield point where the linear elastic region ends; the peak (B) is the ultimate strength.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 150" width="280" height="150"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="260" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M30 120 L90 50 Q100 45 115 48 L150 40 Q190 30 210 55 L230 95" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="92" cy="49" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="100" y="44" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">A</text><circle cx="172" cy="33" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="178" y="30" font-size="9" fill="#16a34a">B</text><text x="250" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#475569">strain</text><text x="34" y="14" font-size="10" fill="#475569">stress</text></svg>
figure.caption: Tensile stress–strain curve

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The coefficient of performance (COP) of a refrigerator is:
questions.solution: COP_ref = Q_absorbed/W_input, and can be greater than 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Slow, continuous deformation of a material under constant load at high temperature is called:
questions.solution: Creep is time-dependent plastic strain that becomes significant at high temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A reversed Carnot refrigerator works between 300 K and 270 K. Its COP is about:
questions.solution: COP = T_cold/(T_hot − T_cold) = 270/(300−270) = 9.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Boundary-layer separation on a body occurs primarily because of:
questions.solution: When pressure rises in the flow direction, near-wall fluid decelerates and reverses, separating the layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water (ν = 10⁻⁶ m²/s) flows at 2 m/s in a 0.05 m pipe. The Reynolds number is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pitot tube measures the:
questions.solution: It senses stagnation pressure; the difference from static pressure gives velocity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a converging subsonic nozzle, as area decreases the velocity ____ and pressure ____:
questions.solution: Continuity speeds up the flow; Bernoulli then drops the pressure as velocity rises.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pin-ended column (E = 200 GPa, I = 4×10⁻⁶ m⁴, length 2 m) has an Euler buckling load of about:
questions.solution: P_cr = π²EI/L² = π²×200e9×4e-6/2² ≈ 1973.9 kN.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is INCORRECT for an adiabatic process?
options: The temperature of the gas cannot change
questions.solution: Adiabatic means Q = 0, but expansion/compression changes temperature and internal energy.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Carnot engine works between 700 K and 300 K. Its efficiency is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Resilience and toughness differ because resilience is energy absorbed ____ whereas toughness is energy absorbed ____:
options: Up to fracture; up to the elastic limit
Up to the elastic limit; up to fracture
questions.solution: Resilience = elastic strain energy; toughness = total energy absorbed up to fracture.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Poisson's ratio is the ratio of:
questions.solution: ν = −(lateral strain)/(longitudinal strain); for metals ≈ 0.3.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A solid shaft of 40 mm diameter transmits a torque of 200 N·m. The maximum shear stress is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The velocity ratio of a simple gear train depends on the:
options: Number of teeth on the gears
questions.solution: Speed ratio = N_driver/N_driven = (teeth on driven)/(teeth on driver).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a cycle, 1000 kJ of heat is added and 500 kJ rejected per cycle. The thermal efficiency is:
questions.solution: η = 1 − Q_rejected/Q_added = 1 − 500/1000 = 50%.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On Mohr's circle for plane stress, the principal stresses are located at:
options: The points where the circle crosses the normal-stress axis (shear = 0)
The top and bottom of the circle
questions.solution: Principal stresses occur where shear stress is zero — the circle's intersections with the σ axis.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The heat-treatment process used to soften steel and relieve internal stresses is:
questions.solution: Annealing heats and slowly cools steel, softening it and relieving stresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows through the contracting pipe shown from diameter 10 cm (V₁ = 3 m/s) to 10 cm. The velocity in the smaller section is:
questions.solution: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂ ⇒ V₂ = V₁(d₁/d₂)² = 3×(10/10)² = 3 m/s.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><path d="M20 30 L150 30 L150 45 L280 45 L280 70 L150 70 L150 85 L20 85 Z" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₁=10cm</text><text x="210" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₂=10cm</text><line x1="60" y1="57" x2="100" y2="57" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="100,57 93,53 93,61" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="60" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">V₁=3m/s</text></svg>
figure.caption: Contracting pipe (continuity)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: During throttling of a real gas through a valve, the quantity that stays essentially constant is:
questions.solution: Throttling is isenthalpic; the Joule–Thomson effect generally changes temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows from a 30 cm² pipe (velocity 3 m/s) into a 10 cm² pipe. The new velocity is:
questions.solution: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂ ⇒ V₂ = 30×3/10 = 9 m/s.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an isothermal process the property held constant is:
questions.solution: Iso-thermal means constant temperature.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: If the diameter of a solid circular shaft is doubled (same material, same shear stress), the torque it can transmit increases by a factor of about:
questions.solution: Torque capacity ∝ d³ (T = τ·πd³/16), so doubling d gives 2³ = 8×.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the p–V diagram shown, two expansions start from the same state (V₁ ≈ 1). The steeper curve (1) is the:
questions.solution: Adiabatic curves (pV^γ) are steeper than isothermal curves (pV) on a p–V diagram because γ > 1.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 260 150" width="260" height="150"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="240" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M50 30 C 90 70 140 100 220 112" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><path d="M50 45 C 95 85 150 108 220 118" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/><text x="150" y="60" font-size="9" fill="#2563eb">curve 1 (steeper)</text><text x="150" y="100" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">curve 2 (flatter)</text><text x="232" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#475569">V</text><text x="34" y="14" font-size="10" fill="#475569">p</text></svg>
figure.caption: p–V diagram: adiabatic vs isothermal

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A venturimeter is primarily used to measure:
questions.solution: It uses the pressure drop at a throat (Bernoulli) to find discharge.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows through the contracting pipe shown from diameter 12 cm (V₁ = 4 m/s) to 10 cm. The velocity in the smaller section is:
questions.solution: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂ ⇒ V₂ = V₁(d₁/d₂)² = 4×(12/10)² = 5.76 m/s.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><path d="M20 30 L150 30 L150 45 L280 45 L280 70 L150 70 L150 85 L20 85 Z" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₁=12cm</text><text x="210" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₂=10cm</text><line x1="60" y1="57" x2="100" y2="57" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="100,57 93,53 93,61" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="60" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">V₁=4m/s</text></svg>
figure.caption: Contracting pipe (continuity)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A four-stroke engine completes one power stroke every:
questions.solution: Suction, compression, power and exhaust occupy two crankshaft revolutions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The COP of a reversed Carnot refrigerator working between T₁ (hot) and T₂ (cold) is:
questions.solution: COP_ref = T_cold/(T_hot − T_cold), with absolute temperatures.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the simply supported beam shown (central load W = 30 kN, span L = 4 m), the maximum bending moment is:
questions.solution: For a central point load, M_max = WL/4 = 30×4/4 = 30 kN·m.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><line x1="30" y1="60" x2="270" y2="60" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="3"/><polygon points="30,60 22,78 38,78" fill="none" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><polygon points="270,60 262,78 278,78" fill="none" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="150" y1="20" x2="150" y2="58" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="150,58 145,48 155,48" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="150" y="16" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#dc2626">W=30kN</text><text x="150" y="96" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">span L=4 m (load at mid-span)</text></svg>
figure.caption: Simply supported beam, central load

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Enthalpy h is defined as:
questions.solution: Enthalpy h = u + pv, a convenient property for flow processes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A specimen shows a stress of 200 MPa at a strain of 0.001. Its Young's modulus is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Adding a fin to a surface improves heat dissipation only when the fin's:
questions.solution: A fin helps only if it transfers more heat than the bare base area it covers (effectiveness > 1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bernoulli's equation is fundamentally a statement of:
options: Conservation of energy along a streamline
questions.solution: It balances pressure, kinetic and potential energy per unit weight along a streamline (no losses).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows at 3 m/s through a pipe of area 0.02 m². The volume flow rate is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The endurance (fatigue) limit of a material is the stress below which it:
options: Can endure virtually infinite load cycles
questions.solution: Below the endurance limit, ferrous materials survive an effectively unlimited number of cycles.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A shaft transmits a torque of 200 N·m at 600 rpm. The power transmitted is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Rankine cycle is the ideal cycle for a:
questions.solution: The Rankine cycle (boiler–turbine–condenser–pump) models vapour power plants.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A sudden change of cross-section in a loaded part causes:
questions.solution: Geometric discontinuities raise local stress well above the nominal value.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A driver gear (30 teeth) at 1200 rpm meshes with a 40-tooth gear. The driven gear speed is:
questions.solution: N_out = N_in·(T_driver/T_driven) = 1200×30/40 = 900 rpm.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The bending stress in a beam varies with distance y from the neutral axis as:
questions.solution: Flexure formula σ = My/I; stress is maximum at the outermost fibre.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An Otto-cycle engine has a compression ratio of 6 (γ = 1.4). Its air-standard efficiency is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum-shear-stress (Tresca) theory of failure compares the maximum shear stress with:
options: Half the yield stress in simple tension
questions.solution: Tresca predicts yielding when τ_max = σ_yield/2 (the shear at yield in a tensile test).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Pascal's law states that pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted:
questions.solution: Pressure in a confined static fluid acts equally in every direction — basis of hydraulics.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the simply supported beam shown (central load W = 20 kN, span L = 6 m), the maximum bending moment is:
questions.solution: For a central point load, M_max = WL/4 = 20×6/4 = 30 kN·m.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><line x1="30" y1="60" x2="270" y2="60" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="3"/><polygon points="30,60 22,78 38,78" fill="none" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><polygon points="270,60 262,78 278,78" fill="none" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="1.5"/><line x1="150" y1="20" x2="150" y2="58" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="150,58 145,48 155,48" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="150" y="16" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#dc2626">W=20kN</text><text x="150" y="96" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">span L=6 m (load at mid-span)</text></svg>
figure.caption: Simply supported beam, central load

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about engine cycles is INCORRECT?
options: Increasing the compression ratio decreases the Otto-cycle efficiency
Otto efficiency rises with compression ratio
Diesel engines use higher compression ratios than petrol engines
Knock limits the compression ratio of SI engines
questions.solution: η_Otto = 1 − 1/r^(γ−1) increases with r — so the 'decreases' statement is wrong.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A refrigerator extracts 400 W of heat using 50 W of work. Its COP is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The function of a governor is to:
options: Keep the mean speed nearly constant under varying load
questions.solution: A governor adjusts fuel/working-fluid supply to maintain mean speed as load changes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Hooke's law states that, within the elastic limit, stress is:
questions.solution: σ = Eε within the elastic range; E is Young's modulus.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A wall (k = 200 W/m·K, area 2 m², thickness 0.05 m) has a 150 K temperature difference. The conduction rate is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For a flow with Mach number greater than 1, the flow is described as:
questions.solution: Mach number M = V/a; M > 1 is supersonic, where compressibility effects dominate.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: On the tensile stress–strain curve shown for mild steel (yield ≈ 350 MPa), point A (end of the initial straight line) represents the:
questions.solution: Point A is the yield point where the linear elastic region ends; the peak (B) is the ultimate strength.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 280 150" width="280" height="150"><line x1="30" y1="120" x2="260" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><line x1="30" y1="15" x2="30" y2="120" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1"/><path d="M30 120 L90 50 Q100 45 115 48 L150 40 Q190 30 210 55 L230 95" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><circle cx="92" cy="49" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="100" y="44" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">A</text><circle cx="172" cy="33" r="4" fill="none" stroke="#16a34a" stroke-width="1.5"/><text x="178" y="30" font-size="9" fill="#16a34a">B</text><text x="250" y="134" font-size="10" fill="#475569">strain</text><text x="34" y="14" font-size="10" fill="#475569">stress</text></svg>
figure.caption: Tensile stress–strain curve

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The first law of thermodynamics is essentially a statement of the conservation of:
questions.solution: ΔU = Q − W; energy is conserved as it changes between heat and work.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The second law of thermodynamics implies that heat, on its own, flows from:
options: A hotter body to a colder body
A colder body to a hotter body
questions.solution: Spontaneous heat flow is from high to low temperature; the reverse needs work (Clausius statement).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the cantilever shown (end load W = 10 kN, length L = 2 m), the maximum bending moment (at the fixed end) is:
questions.solution: For a cantilever with an end load, M_max = WL = 10×2 = 20 kN·m at the fixed end.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><rect x="20" y="35" width="12" height="50" fill="#94a3b8"/><line x1="32" y1="60" x2="260" y2="60" stroke="#1e293b" stroke-width="3"/><line x1="260" y1="22" x2="260" y2="58" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="260,58 255,48 265,48" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="260" y="18" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle" fill="#dc2626">W=10kN</text><text x="150" y="96" font-size="10" text-anchor="middle" fill="#475569">cantilever length L=2 m (load at free end)</text></svg>
figure.caption: Cantilever with end load

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ideal-gas equation of state is:
questions.solution: pV = mRT relates pressure, volume and absolute temperature for an ideal gas.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A cantilever (50×100 mm section) of length 1.5 m carries an end load of 2 kN. The maximum bending stress is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Flow in a circular pipe is generally laminar when the Reynolds number is below about:
questions.solution: Below Re ≈ 2000 pipe flow is laminar; above ~4000 it is turbulent.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Factor of safety is defined as:
questions.solution: FoS = failure stress/allowable working stress; it is always greater than 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows through the contracting pipe shown from diameter 10 cm (V₁ = 4 m/s) to 10 cm. The velocity in the smaller section is:
questions.solution: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂ ⇒ V₂ = V₁(d₁/d₂)² = 4×(10/10)² = 4 m/s.

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 300 110" width="300" height="110"><path d="M20 30 L150 30 L150 45 L280 45 L280 70 L150 70 L150 85 L20 85 Z" fill="none" stroke="#2563eb" stroke-width="2"/><text x="80" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₁=10cm</text><text x="210" y="62" font-size="10" fill="#475569">d₂=10cm</text><line x1="60" y1="57" x2="100" y2="57" stroke="#dc2626" stroke-width="2"/><polygon points="100,57 93,53 93,61" fill="#dc2626"/><text x="60" y="48" font-size="9" fill="#dc2626">V₁=4m/s</text></svg>
figure.caption: Contracting pipe (continuity)

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In a lathe, the principal cutting operation produces a surface of revolution by:
questions.solution: Turning removes material from a rotating workpiece using a single-point tool.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Water flows at 5 m/s through a pipe of area 0.05 m². The volume flow rate is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Euler's critical buckling load of a column is proportional to:
options: EI and inversely to the length
EI and inversely to the square of effective length
questions.solution: P_cr = π²EI/L_e²; it depends on flexural rigidity and end conditions, not directly on yield stress.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For steady incompressible flow in a pipe, the continuity equation gives:
questions.solution: Mass conservation for constant density: A₁V₁ = A₂V₂.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A spring of stiffness 500 N/m is stretched 0.02 m. The restoring force is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The efficiency of a Carnot engine operating between T₁ (hot) and T₂ (cold), in kelvin, is:
questions.solution: Carnot efficiency η = 1 − T₂/T₁ with temperatures in kelvin.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A Carnot engine takes 2000 kJ from a source at 1000 K and rejects to 300 K. The work output is about:
